<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] 22 files with no entry points is expected for a pure test zone; confirm the suite runs in CI via 'ndx ci' to ensure coverage is not silently skipped.
[observation] [info] Cohesion of 1.0 with zero coupling is the gold standard for an e2e test zone — tests interact with the system only through its public CLI surface, not through internal imports.
[observation] [info] The presence of 'architecture-policy.test.js' alongside command-contract tests signals that architectural constraints (tier boundaries, gateway rules) are machine-verified rather than convention-only — a strong quality signal.
[pattern] [warning] e2e-tests zone covers 22 orchestration CLI contracts but domain packages (rex, sourcevision, hench) have no peer e2e zone visible in the zone graph — if domain CLIs are exercised only through unit tests, regressions in their public CLI surface may go undetected until the orchestration layer fails.
[anti-pattern] [warning] No e2e test file targets the 'ndx init' multi-package initialization chain — cli-contract.test.js and cli-delegation.test.js cover delegation but initialization sequencing (sourcevision init → rex init → hench init) has no dedicated coverage, leaving cross-package init ordering regressions undetected.
[anti-pattern] [warning] The e2e suite covers orchestration commands exhaustively but hench's autonomous agent loop (ndx work) is tested only through cli-arg-contracts — no e2e test validates end-to-end task selection, brief construction, and LLM dispatch, meaning the highest-value user path has the weakest integration coverage.
[suggestion] [warning] The MCP HTTP transport layer (ndx start + /mcp/rex + /mcp/sourcevision endpoints) has zero e2e coverage — a cli-mcp.test.js that boots the server and validates session creation, tool listing, and a sample tool invocation would close the most significant uncovered surface in the e2e suite
[suggestion] [info] architecture-policy.test.js conflates CLI contract validation with architectural invariant enforcement — these are distinct failure modes (CLI flags vs import rules) that belong in separate CI gates; consider moving architectural enforcement to a fast lint-time check rather than a slow e2e spawn
[suggestion] [warning] e2e tests are authored in .js while domain package tests are .ts — converting the e2e suite to TypeScript would eliminate a type-checking gap at the layer that exercises the highest-risk cross-package integration paths; the spawn-based test pattern (no production imports) means migration risk is low

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling (0.0) confirm this zone is fully self-contained — e2e tests import nothing from production source and rely solely on spawned processes.
- Coverage spans all orchestration commands (ci, config, delegation, dev, errors, start, status, sync, usage, work) plus architecture policy enforcement, indicating mature contract-level test coverage.
- Zero coupling means e2e tests are never accidentally pulled into production bundles and cannot create circular dependency hazards — a clean boundary worth preserving.
- Cohesion of 1.0 with zero coupling is the gold standard for an e2e test zone — tests interact with the system only through its public CLI surface, not through internal imports.
- The presence of 'architecture-policy.test.js' alongside command-contract tests signals that architectural constraints (tier boundaries, gateway rules) are machine-verified rather than convention-only — a strong quality signal.
- 22 files with no entry points is expected for a pure test zone; confirm the suite runs in CI via 'ndx ci' to ensure coverage is not silently skipped.
- The e2e-tests zone validates orchestration-tier CLI contracts exclusively — no domain-package (rex, sourcevision, hench) e2e zones are present in the overall zone graph, suggesting contract-level coverage asymmetry: orchestration commands are machine-verified but domain CLIs are tested only through unit/integration layers.
- e2e-tests zone covers 22 orchestration CLI contracts but domain packages (rex, sourcevision, hench) have no peer e2e zone visible in the zone graph — if domain CLIs are exercised only through unit tests, regressions in their public CLI surface may go undetected until the orchestration layer fails.
- The e2e-tests zone is the only layer that exercises cross-package integration paths (sourcevision → rex → hench chain) without importing from any package — if the orchestration chain regresses silently (e.g. sourcevision output format changes and rex analyze breaks), e2e-tests is the last line of detection before users are affected.
- Zero coupling combined with 22-file breadth means new orchestration commands added to cli.js without a corresponding e2e test create undetected contract gaps; the zone's perfect cohesion masks this omission risk because the metric cannot distinguish covered commands from uncovered ones.
- The presence of 'cli-delegation.test.js' implies the e2e suite verifies that orchestration commands delegate correctly to domain CLIs rather than reimplementing logic — this delegation contract test is a high-value safeguard for the spawn-only architectural rule enforced in CLAUDE.md.
- No e2e test file targets the 'ndx init' multi-package initialization chain — cli-contract.test.js and cli-delegation.test.js cover delegation but initialization sequencing (sourcevision init → rex init → hench init) has no dedicated coverage, leaving cross-package init ordering regressions undetected.
- The e2e suite covers orchestration commands exhaustively but hench's autonomous agent loop (ndx work) is tested only through cli-arg-contracts — no e2e test validates end-to-end task selection, brief construction, and LLM dispatch, meaning the highest-value user path has the weakest integration coverage.
- architecture-policy.test.js breaks the cli-*.test.js naming convention used by all other 21 files — it tests architectural invariants (import rules, tier boundaries), not a CLI command surface, making it semantically distinct from every other file in the zone
- No cli-mcp.test.js exists — the MCP HTTP transport endpoints (/mcp/rex, /mcp/sourcevision) exposed by ndx start are a significant server-side surface with zero e2e coverage; all existing tests exercise CLI spawn paths only
- The e2e test files use .js extensions while all domain package test suites (rex, sourcevision, hench, llm-client) use .ts — e2e tests bypass TypeScript type checking despite covering the highest-risk cross-package integration paths in the monorepo
- architecture-policy.test.js conflates CLI contract validation with architectural invariant enforcement — these are distinct failure modes (CLI flags vs import rules) that belong in separate CI gates; consider moving architectural enforcement to a fast lint-time check rather than a slow e2e spawn
- The MCP HTTP transport layer (ndx start + /mcp/rex + /mcp/sourcevision endpoints) has zero e2e coverage — a cli-mcp.test.js that boots the server and validates session creation, tool listing, and a sample tool invocation would close the most significant uncovered surface in the e2e suite
- e2e tests are authored in .js while domain package tests are .ts — converting the e2e suite to TypeScript would eliminate a type-checking gap at the layer that exercises the highest-risk cross-package integration paths; the spawn-based test pattern (no production imports) means migration risk is low
- [call graph] 447 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
